instruction: Show how to write table-driven tests in Go and explain why this pattern is idiomatic. Include subtests for better test output.
output:
<think>
Table-driven tests are the idiomatic Go testing pattern because they separate test data from test logic. You define a slice of test cases (each with input, expected output, and a descriptive name), then loop over them. This makes it trivial to add new test cases — just append to the slice. Combined with t.Run() subtests, each case gets its own name in test output, making failures easy to identify. The pattern also works well with t.Parallel() for concurrent test execution. Key design points: use descriptive test case names (not 'test1', 'test2'), test both success and failure cases, and use anonymous structs for the test table to keep everything local. The subtest pattern (t.Run) also enables running individual test cases: 'go test -run TestFoo/case_name'. This is much cleaner than writing separate test functions for each case or using if-else chains inside a single test.
</think>

```go
package calc

import (
	"math"
	"testing"
)

// Function under test
func Divide(a, b float64) (float64, error) {
	if b == 0 {
		return 0, ErrDivisionByZero
	}
	return a / b, nil
}

var ErrDivisionByZero = fmt.Errorf("division by zero")

// Table-driven test with subtests
func TestDivide(t *testing.T) {
	tests := []struct {
		name    string
		a, b    float64
		want    float64
		wantErr bool
	}{
		// Success cases
		{name: "positive integers", a: 10, b: 2, want: 5},
		{name: "negative result", a: -10, b: 2, want: -5},
		{name: "fractional result", a: 7, b: 3, want: 7.0 / 3.0},
		{name: "dividend is zero", a: 0, b: 5, want: 0},
		{name: "large numbers", a: 1e15, b: 1e5, want: 1e10},

		// Error cases
		{name: "divide by zero", a: 10, b: 0, wantErr: true},
		{name: "zero divided by zero", a: 0, b: 0, wantErr: true},
	}

	for _, tt := range tests {
		// t.Run creates a named subtest
		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			got, err := Divide(tt.a, tt.b)

			// Check error expectation
			if (err != nil) != tt.wantErr {
				t.Errorf("Divide(%v, %v) error = %v, wantErr %v",
					tt.a, tt.b, err, tt.wantErr)
				return
			}

			if tt.wantErr {
				return // Error expected, don't check value
			}

			// Float comparison with tolerance
			if math.Abs(got-tt.want) > 1e-9 {
				t.Errorf("Divide(%v, %v) = %v, want %v",
					tt.a, tt.b, got, tt.want)
			}
		})
	}
}

// Run specific case: go test -run TestDivide/positive_integers
// Run all:           go test -run TestDivide
// Verbose:           go test -v -run TestDivide

// Output on failure:
// --- FAIL: TestDivide/fractional_result (0.00s)
//     calc_test.go:52: Divide(7, 3) = 2.333, want 2.5

// === PARALLEL subtests ===

func TestDivideParallel(t *testing.T) {
	tests := []struct {
		name string
		a, b float64
		want float64
	}{
		{"case1", 10, 2, 5},
		{"case2", 20, 4, 5},
		{"case3", 100, 10, 10},
	}

	for _, tt := range tests {
		tt := tt // Capture range variable (required for parallel)
		t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			t.Parallel() // Run this subtest concurrently
			got, _ := Divide(tt.a, tt.b)
			if got != tt.want {
				t.Errorf("got %v, want %v", got, tt.want)
			}
		})
	}
}
```

instruction: Show how to write benchmarks and fuzz tests in Go. When should you use each?
output:
<think>
Benchmarks and fuzz tests serve different purposes. Benchmarks measure performance — they run a function b.N times and report ns/op, bytes/op, and allocs/op. You use them to compare implementations, detect performance regressions, and optimize hot paths. The testing framework automatically adjusts b.N to get statistically significant results. Key patterns: use b.ResetTimer() after setup, b.ReportAllocs() for memory info, and b.RunParallel() for concurrent benchmarks. Fuzz testing (Go 1.18+) generates random inputs to find bugs that unit tests miss — crashes, panics, incorrect output for edge cases. The fuzzer starts with a seed corpus (your known inputs) and mutates them, guided by code coverage. It's especially good for parsers, serializers, and any function that handles untrusted input. Fuzz tests found bugs in the Go standard library itself. You run fuzz with -fuzz flag, and it runs until it finds a failure or you stop it. Found failures are saved as test cases in testdata/.
</think>

```go
package stringutil

import (
	"strings"
	"testing"
	"unicode/utf8"
)

// Function under test
func Reverse(s string) string {
	runes := []rune(s)
	for i, j := 0, len(runes)-1; i < j; i, j = i+1, j-1 {
		runes[i], runes[j] = runes[j], runes[i]
	}
	return string(runes)
}

// === BENCHMARKS ===
// Run: go test -bench=. -benchmem

func BenchmarkReverse(b *testing.B) {
	input := strings.Repeat("hello world ", 100)

	b.ReportAllocs() // Report memory allocations
	b.ResetTimer()   // Don't include setup in timing

	for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
		Reverse(input)
	}
}

// Output:
// BenchmarkReverse-8  123456  9876 ns/op  4896 B/op  2 allocs/op

// Benchmark with sub-benchmarks for different sizes
func BenchmarkReverseSize(b *testing.B) {
	sizes := []struct {
		name string
		size int
	}{
		{"small", 10},
		{"medium", 1000},
		{"large", 100000},
	}

	for _, s := range sizes {
		input := strings.Repeat("a", s.size)
		b.Run(s.name, func(b *testing.B) {
			for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
				Reverse(input)
			}
		})
	}
}

// Parallel benchmark — test under concurrency
func BenchmarkReverseParallel(b *testing.B) {
	input := "concurrency test string"

	b.RunParallel(func(pb *testing.PB) {
		for pb.Next() {
			Reverse(input)
		}
	})
}

// === FUZZ TESTING (Go 1.18+) ===
// Run: go test -fuzz=FuzzReverse -fuzztime=30s

func FuzzReverse(f *testing.F) {
	// Seed corpus: known inputs to start with
	f.Add("hello")
	f.Add("")
	f.Add("a")
	f.Add("ab")
	f.Add("\xbd\xb2")  // Invalid UTF-8
	f.Add("Hello, \u4e16\u754c") // Unicode

	f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, input string) {
		// Property 1: Reversing twice gives original
		result := Reverse(input)
		doubleReversed := Reverse(result)
		if input != doubleReversed {
			t.Errorf("Reverse(Reverse(%q)) = %q, want %q",
				input, doubleReversed, input)
		}

		// Property 2: Length is preserved (in runes)
		if utf8.RuneCountInString(result) != utf8.RuneCountInString(input) {
			t.Errorf("rune count changed: input=%d, reversed=%d",
				utf8.RuneCountInString(input),
				utf8.RuneCountInString(result))
		}

		// Property 3: Valid UTF-8 stays valid
		if utf8.ValidString(input) && !utf8.ValidString(result) {
			t.Errorf("Reverse(%q) produced invalid UTF-8: %q", input, result)
		}
	})
}

// If fuzzer finds a bug, it saves to testdata/fuzz/FuzzReverse/<hash>
// The failing input becomes a permanent regression test

// WHEN TO USE:
// Benchmarks: performance-critical code, comparing implementations,
//             detecting regressions (run in CI with benchstat)
// Fuzz tests: parsers, encoders/decoders, any untrusted input handler,
//             finding edge cases humans miss (unicode, empty, huge)
```

instruction: Show how to use test fixtures and test helpers in Go. How do you set up and tear down test state properly?
output:
<think>
Go testing doesn't have built-in setup/teardown like JUnit's @Before/@After, but it provides TestMain for package-level setup and t.Cleanup() for per-test cleanup. TestMain runs before any tests and lets you do expensive one-time setup (starting a database, loading fixtures). t.Cleanup registers functions that run after the test completes (even if it fails or panics), which is Go's idiomatic way to handle teardown. Test helpers are functions that start with t.Helper() — this marks them so that when they call t.Errorf, the error points to the caller's line, not the helper's line. This is crucial for readable test output. For temporary files, t.TempDir() creates a directory that's automatically cleaned up. For test data files, the convention is a testdata/ directory which go build ignores. A common pattern is a factory function that creates test state and returns a cleanup function.
</think>

```go
package store

import (
	"os"
	"path/filepath"
	"testing"
)

// === TEST HELPERS: t.Helper() for clean error reporting ===

// Helper marks this function — errors report caller's line, not helper's
func assertEqual(t *testing.T, got, want interface{}) {
	t.Helper() // Without this, errors point HERE instead of the caller
	if got != want {
		t.Errorf("got %v, want %v", got, want)
	}
}

func assertNoError(t *testing.T, err error) {
	t.Helper()
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("unexpected error: %v", err)
	}
}

func assertError(t *testing.T, err error, contains string) {
	t.Helper()
	if err == nil {
		t.Fatal("expected error, got nil")
	}
	if !strings.Contains(err.Error(), contains) {
		t.Errorf("error %q should contain %q", err.Error(), contains)
	}
}

// === TEST FIXTURES: Factory with cleanup ===

type TestStore struct {
	Dir   string
	Store *FileStore
}

func setupTestStore(t *testing.T) *TestStore {
	t.Helper()

	// t.TempDir() auto-cleans up after test
	dir := t.TempDir()

	store, err := NewFileStore(dir)
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("failed to create store: %v", err)
	}

	// t.Cleanup runs after test completes (even on failure)
	t.Cleanup(func() {
		if err := store.Close(); err != nil {
			t.Errorf("failed to close store: %v", err)
		}
	})

	// Seed test data
	store.Put("key1", []byte("value1"))
	store.Put("key2", []byte("value2"))

	return &TestStore{Dir: dir, Store: store}
}

// === TESTS USING FIXTURES ===

func TestStore_Get(t *testing.T) {
	ts := setupTestStore(t) // Setup + automatic cleanup

	val, err := ts.Store.Get("key1")
	assertNoError(t, err)
	assertEqual(t, string(val), "value1")
}

func TestStore_GetMissing(t *testing.T) {
	ts := setupTestStore(t)

	_, err := ts.Store.Get("nonexistent")
	assertError(t, err, "not found")
}

func TestStore_Delete(t *testing.T) {
	ts := setupTestStore(t)

	assertNoError(t, ts.Store.Delete("key1"))

	_, err := ts.Store.Get("key1")
	assertError(t, err, "not found")
}

// === TESTMAIN: Package-level setup/teardown ===

func TestMain(m *testing.M) {
	// One-time setup (database, external services, etc.)
	setupPackage()

	// Run all tests
	code := m.Run()

	// One-time teardown
	teardownPackage()

	os.Exit(code)
}

func setupPackage()    { /* start test DB, load schemas */ }
func teardownPackage() { /* stop test DB, clean up */ }

// === GOLDEN FILES: testdata/ directory ===

func TestRender(t *testing.T) {
	got := renderTemplate("test input")

	golden := filepath.Join("testdata", t.Name()+".golden")

	if *update {
		// go test -update — regenerate golden files
		os.WriteFile(golden, []byte(got), 0644)
	}

	want, err := os.ReadFile(golden)
	assertNoError(t, err)
	assertEqual(t, got, string(want))
}

var update = flag.Bool("update", false, "update golden files")
```

instruction: How do you structure test code for complex Go services that depend on databases, HTTP APIs, and other external systems?
output:
<think>
Testing complex Go services requires interfaces and dependency injection to isolate components. The key principle: define interfaces for external dependencies (database, HTTP client, cache), then provide mock implementations in tests. Go's implicit interface satisfaction makes this natural — you don't need mock frameworks, just struct implementations that satisfy the interface. For HTTP servers, httptest.NewServer creates a real HTTP server on localhost for integration tests. For database tests, you can use an in-memory implementation, a test container, or the interface-mock approach. The testing pyramid applies: many fast unit tests with mocks, fewer integration tests with real dependencies, and very few end-to-end tests. A common pattern is the testify/mock library or hand-rolled mocks. I prefer hand-rolled mocks because they're type-safe and don't require code generation, but for large interfaces, mockgen saves time.
</think>

```go
package service

import (
	"context"
	"fmt"
	"net/http"
	"net/http/httptest"
	"testing"
)

// === INTERFACES for testability ===

type UserRepository interface {
	GetUser(ctx context.Context, id string) (*User, error)
	SaveUser(ctx context.Context, user *User) error
}

type EmailSender interface {
	Send(ctx context.Context, to, subject, body string) error
}

type User struct {
	ID    string
	Name  string
	Email string
}

// Service depends on interfaces, not concrete types
type UserService struct {
	repo  UserRepository
	email EmailSender
}

func NewUserService(repo UserRepository, email EmailSender) *UserService {
	return &UserService{repo: repo, email: email}
}

func (s *UserService) Register(ctx context.Context, user *User) error {
	if err := s.repo.SaveUser(ctx, user); err != nil {
		return fmt.Errorf("save user: %w", err)
	}
	if err := s.email.Send(ctx, user.Email, "Welcome", "Hello "+user.Name); err != nil {
		// Log but don't fail — email is not critical
		fmt.Printf("warning: email failed: %v\n", err)
	}
	return nil
}

// === MOCK IMPLEMENTATIONS ===

type mockRepo struct {
	users    map[string]*User
	saveCalls int
	saveErr   error
}

func newMockRepo() *mockRepo {
	return &mockRepo{users: make(map[string]*User)}
}

func (m *mockRepo) GetUser(_ context.Context, id string) (*User, error) {
	user, ok := m.users[id]
	if !ok {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("user %s not found", id)
	}
	return user, nil
}

func (m *mockRepo) SaveUser(_ context.Context, user *User) error {
	m.saveCalls++
	if m.saveErr != nil {
		return m.saveErr
	}
	m.users[user.ID] = user
	return nil
}

type mockEmail struct {
	sent    []string
	sendErr error
}

func (m *mockEmail) Send(_ context.Context, to, subject, body string) error {
	if m.sendErr != nil {
		return m.sendErr
	}
	m.sent = append(m.sent, to)
	return nil
}

// === UNIT TESTS with mocks ===

func TestUserService_Register(t *testing.T) {
	repo := newMockRepo()
	email := &mockEmail{}
	svc := NewUserService(repo, email)

	user := &User{ID: "1", Name: "Alice", Email: "alice@test.com"}
	err := svc.Register(context.Background(), user)

	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("unexpected error: %v", err)
	}
	if repo.saveCalls != 1 {
		t.Errorf("expected 1 save call, got %d", repo.saveCalls)
	}
	if len(email.sent) != 1 || email.sent[0] != "alice@test.com" {
		t.Errorf("expected email to alice@test.com, got %v", email.sent)
	}
}

func TestUserService_Register_DBError(t *testing.T) {
	repo := newMockRepo()
	repo.saveErr = fmt.Errorf("connection refused")
	svc := NewUserService(repo, &mockEmail{})

	err := svc.Register(context.Background(), &User{ID: "1"})
	if err == nil {
		t.Fatal("expected error")
	}
}

// === HTTP INTEGRATION TEST with httptest ===

func TestAPIHandler(t *testing.T) {
	repo := newMockRepo()
	repo.users["42"] = &User{ID: "42", Name: "Bob", Email: "bob@test.com"}
	svc := NewUserService(repo, &mockEmail{})

	// Real HTTP server on random port
	handler := NewAPIHandler(svc)
	server := httptest.NewServer(handler)
	defer server.Close()

	// Make real HTTP request
	resp, err := http.Get(server.URL + "/users/42")
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("request failed: %v", err)
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()

	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
		t.Errorf("status = %d, want 200", resp.StatusCode)
	}
}

func NewAPIHandler(svc *UserService) http.Handler {
	mux := http.NewServeMux()
	mux.HandleFunc("/users/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
		w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
	})
	return mux
}
```
